title: Coverage of essential health services
subtitle: Coverage of essential health services is measured as an index on a scale of 0 to 100 (where higher is better). This index is based on risk-standardized death rates – which give a measure of healthcare access and quality – and whether common interventions are carried out.
caption: The 2023 global map of essential health service coverage displays national performance on a 0-to-100 index, where darker blue shades represent higher scores based on risk-standardized death rates and the delivery of common health interventions. Across the world, the highest coverage levels — falling in the 80-to-100 band — are concentrated in North America (United States, Canada), Western and Northern Europe (Germany, France, Sweden, Norway), East Asia (Japan, South Korea), and Oceania (Australia, New Zealand). In Latin America, Chile and Argentina sit in the upper-middle range, while Brazil and Mexico fall into the 60–80 band. Across much of sub-Saharan Africa — including Niger, Chad, Somalia, and the Central African Republic — coverage remains in the lowest visible band, between 0 and 40, with some countries shaded in the lightest green tones indicating scores below 20. Parts of South Asia, such as Afghanistan and Pakistan, also appear in the lower bands, alongside Yemen and Haiti in the Caribbean. Intermediate values — roughly 40 to 60 — are visible in countries like India, Indonesia, Egypt, and Nigeria. Greenland and a few small island states are marked as having no data. The geographic distribution shows a clear gradient: high-income regions cluster at the top end of the scale, while low-income regions, particularly in central and western Africa, occupy the bottom. The most consistent visible pattern is the concentration of the lowest coverage scores across the Sahel and Horn of Africa, contrasted with near-universal high scores across industrialized nations in Europe, North America, and East Asia.
